coredata.dc:title: Electric and optoelectronic balances of silicon photodetectors coupled with colloid carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Hsiao, Po-Hsuan
Gao, Zi-Xi
Juan, Joon-Ching
Lin, Yu-Pin
Chen, Sung-Yu
Chen, Chia-Yun
coredata.dc:description: We present the efficient ultraviolet photodetectors made with the incorporation of colloid carbon nanodot (CCND) layers with silicon substrates. By investigating the morphologies, microstructure, light absorption and carrier dynamics, the control over CCND thickness is envisioned for the improvement of photosensing characteristics. We find that 35-nm thick CCNDs coupled with Si can reach remarkable sensing responsivity of 15.9 A/W and detectivity of 2.94 × 1014 Jones under detecting lights with wavelength of 352 nm. The underlying mechanism is interpreted by the synergetic contributions from reduced dynamic dark currents and efficient charge separation from CCND/Si heterojunction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colloid nanodots
Surfaces
Photodetectors
Optical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic architecture of CCND/Si photodetectors. (b) Cross-sectional SEM image of CCND on Si. HRTEM image of a CCND is presented in the inset of Fig. 1(a). (c) CCND thickness versus cycles of coating. (d) UV/Vis absorption results of CCNDs.
(a) TRPL examinations of various samples. Dependence of (b) carrier concentration evaluated with Hall-effect measurement and (c) film resistance on CCND thickness.
(a) Dynamic dark currents and (b) photocurrents of five various CCND/Si photodetectors. Comparisons of device (c) responsivity and (d) detectivity of various samples.

head.ce:title: Electric and optoelectronic balances of silicon photodetectors coupled with colloid carbon nanodots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We present the efficient ultraviolet photodetectors made with the incorporation of colloid carbon nanodot (CCND) layers with silicon substrates. By investigating the morphologies, microstructure, light absorption and carrier dynamics, the control over CCND thickness is envisioned for the improvement of photosensing characteristics. We find that 35-nm thick CCNDs coupled with Si can reach remarkable sensing responsivity of 15.9 A/W and detectivity of 2.94 × 1014 Jones under detecting lights with wavelength of 352 nm. The underlying mechanism is interpreted by the synergetic contributions from reduced dynamic dark currents and efficient charge separation from CCND/Si heterojunction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Silicon (Si) photonics, one of extensive optoelectronic aspects through implementing various optically functional Si components, have attracted worldwide attention for the development of next-generation optoelectronics [1]. Owing from their revolutionized advantages such as CMOS-compatibility, low cost and facile manufacturability, Si photonics have permitted to offer the great potentials for integrating multiple optical components on a single chip. Photodetector is one of the basic building blocks of photonic platform that enables to remedy the conversion of optical inputs to electrical signals. Nevertheless, the essentially high light reflectivity and indirect band nature of Si limit the photodetection performance of Si-based photodetectors toward ultraviolet (UV) regions [2]. Motivated by the need of developing Si-based PDs, several approaches were proposed to date. For instance, plasmonic UV photodetector has been developed for improving the photonic capability for UV utilization [3,4]. Besides, incorporation of nickel oxide nanoparticles with Si has demonstrated the photosensing characteristics up to UV regions with responsivitiy of 4.8 mA/W [5]. While the spectral photodetection response of those Si-based PDs are successfully extended to UV regions, the involved processing complexity including high-vacuum requirement, sophisticated materials deposition and expensive manufacturing limit their practical assessment for large-area production. Recently, the hybrid CsPbBr3/Si based photodetectors fabricated by solution process seem to be promising for bridging the UV photosensing applications, but the correlated detection characteristic in terms of responsivity greatly drops (<0.1 A/W) while the light wavelengths are less than 380 nm [6]. Herein, we demonstrate a compelling Si-based photodetectors coupled with colloid carbon nanodots (CCNDs) functioning as efficient UV absorber, showing the effective UV detection performances with responsivity of 15.9 A/W through the creation of heterojunctions.

Formation of Si-based photodetectors was prepared by dropping aqueous CCNDs in water onto n-type single-crystalline Si substrate (resistivity = 1–10 Ω cm), and subsequently the as-deposited samples were calcined in air atmosphere at 120 °C for 1 hr. Top and rear electrodes were formed by depositing 150 nm of Ag layer and 100 nm of Al layer via electron gun evaporation, respectively.

Fig. 1 (a) illustrates the device structures made by the incorporation of CCND films on n-type Si substrates. By examining the film uniformity, the correlated cross-sectional SEM image is shown in Fig. 1(b), where the uniform CCND layers with densely stacked CCNDs is evidenced. Microstructures of a sole CCND can be also observed in the inset of Fig. 1(b), which indicate the well-crystalline core with lattice fringe of 0.34 nm corresponding to (002) features of graphite structures [7]. To explore the thickness control over forming various CCND layers on Si, the correlation of deposition cycles with respect to the thickness of CCND films is displayed, as shown in Fig. 1(c). By tuning the cycles of drop coating process, the linear correspondence with high regression ratio of 0.998 regarding the CCND thickness in the range of 10 ± 2–60 ± 3 nm evidences that the control over layer thickness can be realized, which acts as a significant role on tailoring the photodetection characteristics of CCND/Si heterostructures. In addition, the light-absorption features of CCNDs are presented in Fig. 1(d), where the results indicate the CCNDs exhibit the strong UV absorption capability while the light wavelengths are less than 370 nm. The two predominant absorption peaks located at 228 nm and 282 nm are assigned to be π to π* transition of CC and n to π* transition of CO, respectively. Accordingly, the Tauc plot presented in the inset of Fig. 1(d) disclosures the band gap energy of CCNDs is 3.23 eV, which corresponds with the literature [8].

TRPL examinations of CCND/n-type Si heterostructures with five various CCND thickness are demonstrated in Fig. 2 (a), where the detailed results are presented in SI. By fitting the dynamic behaviors of PL intensities, we find the minority carrier lifetime (τ) of photoexcited carriers strongly correlates with CCND thickness, where the evaluated τ are 21.6 ns (14 nm of CCNDs) and 45.2 ns (46 nm of CCNDs), respectively. These findings suggest that the increase of CCND thickness causes the long diffusion distance of holes within CCNDs to reach interfaces, which can facilitate the carrier separation, and thus elongates the lifetime of photogenerated electrons. Another determinate phenomenon is associated with the variation of electric characteristics via couplings of CCND layers with Si substrates. Both of carrier concentrations and surface resistances of CCND layers on n-type Si are measured, as demonstrated in Fig. 2(b) and 2(c), respectively. The results clarify that the creation of p-n heterojunction between p-type CCNDs and n-type Si plays the significant role on varying the hole concentrations within CCNDs. When CCNDs are in contact with Si, the fermi energies are balanced through the hole diffusion from CCNDs to Si and electron diffusion from Si to CCNDs, which creates the depletion region across junction that facilitates the charge separation. These explain the carrier concentrations of CCNDs increase from 3.4 × 1012 cm−3 (CCNDs = 14 nm), 1.4 × 1013 cm−3 (CCNDs = 35 nm) to 3.2 × 1014 cm−3 (CCNDs = 58 nm) [Fig. 2(b)], and the substantial reduction of CCND resistance from 1.9 × 104 (CCNDs = 14 nm), 8.1 × 103 (CCNDs = 35 nm) to 2.9 × 103 (CCNDs = 58 nm) [Fig. 2(c)]. It should be noted that high carrier concentration in absorber layers may lead to the formation of leakage currents that degrades the detection detectivity of photodetectors [9], and thus the balance of electric and optoelectronic characteristics is essential for the optimization of UV photodetection of CCND/Si-based devices.

Fig. 3 (a) compares the dark currents of various CCND/Si devices under the examinations of 200 s. All the photodetectors demonstrate the reliably time-domain device characteristics due to the fact that no fluctuation of dark currents can be observed. It can be noted that the involved dark currents reach the highest and lowest in the case of CCNDs = 58 nm and 14 nm among the tested samples, respectively, and the trend corresponds to the results of carrier concentration, as shown in Fig. 2(b). In addition, the explorations on photocurrents excited by the lights with wavelength of 352 nm reveal that the highest photocurrents are achieved when the CCND thickness is 35 nm. This can be attributed to the superior carrier lifetime driven by the efficient charge separation of photogenerated carriers, as evidenced in Fig. 2(a). However, the photocurrents are reduced when the CCND thickness reaches 58 nm. When the UV lights are illuminated to the photodetectors, light absorptions are occurred within CCNDs [Fig. 1(d)], the photogenerated electrons can diffuse to the Si side driven by the built-in field from depletion region, and are eventually collected by Al electrode that contribute to the photocurrents. In this consideration, the comparably thick CCND layers (thickness = 58 nm), the photoexcited electrons may spatially exist far from depletion region, and thus the efficiency for forming photocurrents turns to be comparably low. This explains the intermediate CCND thickness incorporated with Si can contribute to the highest photoresponse. Finally, the sensing responsivity and detectivity, associated with two critical photodetector performances, are compared, as shown in Fig. 3(c) and 3(d), respectively. It can be observed that Si coupled with 35-nm thick CCNDs can reach the highest responsivity (15.9 A/W) among the tested devices, where the results are even superior than graphene/Si [10], ZnO/Si [11] and GaN/Si [12] based photodetectors [11]. In addition, the CCND (35 nm)/Si photodetectors further possess the highest detectivity (2.94 × 1014 Jones) that are potentially feasible for the detection of weak UV light, where this can be attributed to the synergetic contributions from low dark currents and efficient photocurrent excitation.

We successfully demonstrate the formation of CCNDs directly on Si substrates that facilitates the creation of p-n heterojunction. By adjusting the CCND thickness, along with detailed examinations on the morphological, microstructural, UV-absorption features and carrier dynamics, it is found that the incorporation of 35-nm thick CCNDs with Si can achieve detection responsivity of 15.9 A/W and detectivity of 2.94 × 1014 Jones for reliably sensing 352-nm UV lights. We anticipate that the correlated photodetector design along with processing controllability can pave potential ways for impacting the UV detection, manipulation and other function applications.

Po-Hsuan Hsiao: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Zi-Xi Gao: Investigation, Visualization. Joon-Ching Juan: Conceptualization. Yu-Pin Lin: Writing – review & editing. Sung-Yu Chen: Supervision. Chia-Yun Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 110-2223-E-006-003-MY3). In addition, the financial support provided by Bureau of Energy (Grant No. 111-S0102) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.133857.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silicon photonics circuit design: methods, tools and challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Bogaerts, L. Chrostowski, Silicon photonics circuit design: methods, tools and challenges, Laser Photonics Rev. 12 (2018) 1700237.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparative study of silicon-based ultraviolet photodetectors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Shi, S. Nihtianov, Comparative study of silicon-based ultraviolet photodetectors, IEEE Sens. J. 12 (2012) 2453-2459.
H. Chen, K. Liu, L. Hu, A.A. Al-Ghamdi, X. Fang, New concept ultraviolet photodetectors, Mater. Today, 18 (2015) 493-502.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hot electron generation by aluminum oligomers in plasmonic ultraviolet photodetectors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Ahmadivand, R. Sinha, P.K. Vabbina, M. Karabiyik, S. Kaya, N. Pala, Hot electron generation by aluminum oligomers in plasmonic ultraviolet photodetectors, Opt. Express, 24 (2016) 13665-13678.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Farbication of UV photodetector from nickel oxide nanoparticles deposited on silicon substrate by closed-field unbalanced dual magnetron sputtering techniques

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hammadi, M.K. Khalaf, F.J. Kadhim, Farbication of UV photodetector from nickel oxide nanoparticles deposited on silicon substrate by closed-field unbalanced dual magnetron sputtering techniques, Opt. Quantum Electron. 47 (2015) 3805-3813.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-speed colour-converting photodetector with all-inorganic CsPbBr 3 perovskite nanocrystals for ultraviolet light communication

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.H. Kang, I. Dursun, G. Liu, L. Sinatra, X. Sun, M. Kong, J. Pan, P. Maity, E.-N. Ooi, T.K. Ng, High-speed colour-converting photodetector with all-inorganic CsPbBr3 perovskite nanocrystals for ultraviolet light communication, Light Sci. Appl. 8 (2019) 1-12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Well incorporation of carbon nanodots with silicon nanowire arrays featuring excellent photocatalytic performances

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.-Y. Chen, P.-H. Hsiao, T.-C. Wei, T.-C. Chen, C.-H. Tang, Well incorporation of carbon nanodots with silicon nanowire arrays featuring excellent photocatalytic performances, Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 19 (2017) 11786-11792.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blue photoluminescence from chemically derived graphene oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Eda, Y.Y. Lin, C. Mattevi, H. Yamaguchi, H.A. Chen, I.S. Chen, C.W. Chen, M. Chhowalla, Blue photoluminescence from chemically derived graphene oxide, Adv. Mater. 22 (2010) 505-509.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-sized carbon quantum dots enabling outstanding silicon-based photodetectors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.-H. Hsiao, Y.-C. Lai, C.-Y. Chen, Dual-sized carbon quantum dots enabling outstanding silicon-based photodetectors, Appl. Surf. Sci. 542 (2021) 148705.
X. Li, M. Zhu, M. Du, Z. Lv, L. Zhang, Y. Li, Y. Yang, T. Yang, X. Li, K. Wang, High detectivity graphene‐silicon heterojunction photodetector, Small 12 (2016) 595-601.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stability improvement of Cu (ii)-doped ZnS/ZnO photodetectors prepared with a facile solution-processing method

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.-H. Hsiao, T.-C. Wei, C.-Y. Chen, Stability improvement of Cu (ii)-doped ZnS/ZnO photodetectors prepared with a facile solution-processing method, Inorg. Chem. Front. 8 (2021) 311-318.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-powered ultraviolet photodetection realized by GaN/Si nanoheterostructure based on silicon nanoporous pillar array

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.-X. Chen, X.-H. Xiao, Z.-F. Shi, R. Du, X.-J. Li, Self-powered ultraviolet photodetection realized by GaN/Si nanoheterostructure based on silicon nanoporous pillar array, J. Alloy Comp. 767 (2018) 368-373.
